title: Gliricidia maculata
taxonomy: Gliricidia maculata is a species of flowering plant that belongs to the family Fabaceae. It is a member of the subfamily Faboideae and is native to tropical regions of Central and South America. The plant is also known as "Madre de Cacao" and is often used as a shade tree in coffee and cacao plantations. Gliricidia maculata is a deciduous tree that can grow up to 15 meters in height and has compound leaves with 4-10 leaflets. Its classification also includes being a part of the genus Gliricidia.
uses: Gliricidia maculata has been traditionally used in various cultures for its medicinal properties. The plant's leaves and bark are used to treat fever, diarrhea, and other gastrointestinal issues. Its leaves are also used to treat skin conditions such as eczema and dermatitis. In some parts of the world, the plant is used to treat respiratory issues such as bronchitis and asthma. Further research is needed to confirm the effectiveness of Gliricidia maculata's medicinal uses.
benefits: Gliricidia maculata, also known as the Mexican lilac, has been traditionally used in various cultures for its medicinal properties. The plant's leaves and bark are said to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which can be used to treat various health issues. side_effects: Gliricidia maculata is known to contain toxic compounds, including rotenone, which can be hazardous to humans and animals. Ingestion of the plant's leaves or seeds can cause symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain. Prolonged exposure to rotenone can lead to more severe health issues, including liver and kidney damage. The plant's toxicity can also cause skin irritation and allergic reactions in some individuals. It is essential to handle Gliricidia maculata with caution and avoid any direct contact.
intro: Gliricidia maculata, also known as Mexican lilac or butterfly tree, is a small tree native to Central and South America, characterized by its vibrant purple flowers and compound leaves. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits, including treating fever, wounds, and gastrointestinal issues. The bioactive constituents of Gliricidia maculata include alkaloids, flavonoids, and saponins, which are thought to contribute to its medicinal properties.
